title: Setting Up Hybrid Representations
concept: Storing both a dense and sparse representation per document as the foundation for hybrid search later in this phase.

exercises.prompt: Why do we need sparse representations alongside dense ones?

options.text: They excel at finding general synonyms.
They excel at matching exact product IDs and rare words.
They automatically translate words to other languages.

exercises.explain: Sparse representations excel at exact matches like serial numbers or specific IDs, whereas dense embeddings often blur these exact characters together.

cards.title: Two Sides of the Same Coin
cards.body: We know dense vectors capture general meaning, while sparse representations catch exact keywords and codes. But how do we actually combine their powers in our code and databases? Let's build the ultimate search foundation.

cards.title: Hybrid Representations
cards.body: Setting up hybrid representations means saving both a dense vector (for meaning) and a sparse representation (for exact words) under the same document ID in your database. This prepares your database to handle both types of searches at the same time.

cards.title: The Dual-Label Filing Cabinet
cards.body: Think of a library catalog card that has both a summary paragraph (dense) and a list of specific index keywords (sparse). By keeping both on the same physical card, librarians can look up books by their general vibe OR find them using exact, rare terms.

cards.title: A Complete Database Record
cards.body: When storing a product review, you save the raw text, a dense embedding array like [0.12, -0.4, ...], and a sparse dictionary like {"waterproof": 2.5, "goggles": 4.1} in a single database record.

cards.title: The Dual-Database Myth
cards.body: You don't need two separate databases. Modern vector databases allow you to store both dense and sparse representations side-by-side in the very same document record.

cards.title: Double-Lens Storage
cards.body: Storing both data structures in one record allows you to look up documents using two different lenses at the same time.

left.text: Captures abstract concepts, synonyms, and general topics

right.text: Captures exact product IDs, rare jargon, and specific names

exercises.prompt: How are dense and sparse representations typically organized in a modern vector database?

options.text: They must be stored in completely separate databases.
They are stored together in the same document record.
They are merged into a single text paragraph before saving.
The sparse representation is discarded before saving.

exercises.explain: Storing both formats together ensures they stay synchronized, reduces network overhead, and simplifies queries during search.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Storing both representations allows you to perform hybrid search later by combining results from both fields.
exercises.explain: Setting up the database with both fields allows you to run both semantic and keyword queries simultaneously.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the explanation of database storage:
exercises.sentence: To support both semantic and keyword search, you should store a hybrid representation in your database.
exercises.explain: A hybrid representation stores both dense (for meaning) and sparse (for keywords) data in the same document record.

exercises.prompt: Identify which document structure represents a complete hybrid representation ready for search:
exercises.explain: Option C represents a true hybrid setup, containing both a dense vector array (meaning) and a sparse dictionary of token weights (exact match keywords).

exercises.prompt: You are architecting a production hybrid search engine. What is the most efficient and robust database pattern for managing your document representations?

options.text: Store both the dense vector and the sparse token-weight dictionary inside the same database document.
Combine them into a single, massive 10,000-dimension dense vector by adding their values.
Discard the sparse representation and only save the dense representation to save space.
Generate dense embeddings only for headings and sparse representations only for body text.

exercises.explain: Storing both representations in a single database document allows you to query them together or separately, avoiding the mess of trying to sync two different databases.
